title: REVA #8: Phosphotungstic Acid (PTA) Staining of Cadaveric Human Vagus Nerves
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"d4h02","text":"This protocol details the preparation and staining of human cadaveric vagus nerves with phosphotungstic acid (PTA) to provide tissue contrast between the fascicles and surrounding epineurium in computed tomography (CT) imaging. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"2tv82","text":"See the protocols for dissecting (dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.yxmvmb976g3p/v1) and removing (dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.n2bvje9r5gk5/v1) the human vagus nerve from embalmed cadavers, resulting in the vagal complex (from brainstem to abdomen) glued to a series of acrylic boards, each 3 cm wide and up to 9 cm long. The nerve on each acrylic board is termed a “sample”. The acrylic boards are secured with Velcro to a large acrylic sheet in a large container. steps: Line the inside bottom of each container with the Velcro, sufficient to attach the samples.
Place the nerve in its container in the fridge until it’s time for imaging (12 hours (Overnight) to 4 days).
Place the samples in a single container.
Snap the acrylic boards (where they are pre-thinned) between the thoracic and esophageal plexus samples, as described in dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.3byl46w72go5/v1 .

Immediately after cutting, apply a small amount of glue to both ends of the sample to ensure it is stable with minimal movement. Use a dissection probe to gently push the nerve down on top of the glue while it dries.
Cut between the distal-most thoracic sample and proximal-most esophageal plexus on the left and right. Use a fresh razor blade and a fast downward motion (not sawing) for each cut.

Place the cervical and thoracic samples of the nerve in container 1 of 5 and the esophageal-subesophageal samples of the nerve in container 2 of 5. Fill each container with 1 L of 10% formalin or until the nerve is fully submerged and place in the fridge for 12 hours to 4 days.

To prepare for PTA staining, move the cervical and thoracic samples of the nerve into container 3 of 5, and the esophageal-sub esophageal samples of the nerve into container 4 of 5. The samples for one or two cadavers can be placed in a single container.
Fill the container with 1.5 L of 3% PTA (or sufficient volume to fully submerge all nerve sections).
Place the container on a shaker set at 35 rpm, °C, 1 day (cervical and thoracic samples) or 48 hours (esophageal and sub-esophageal samples).
Carefully decant the PTA for waste disposal.
Rinse the nerve with DI water 3 times.
Place the acrylic boards in container 5 of 5.
Cover the nerve in PBS-soaked gauze or cheesecloth.
Replace the containers used for PTA staining after three uses because the PTA degrades the plastic.
creator: Nicole A Pelot
